speaker: interviewer
text: I can't believe that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was trying.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it was my fault, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm going to slide you-- can you slide the whole chair to the right?

words.text: the whole chair to the right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wise leans in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Wise, tell me, when you were in Germany, did you have any children that were born there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Saba. Only one. Marcia was born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when Saba was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in February, the 19th. 1945, 1945, I think, '46, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came over, did you come to America by boat? How did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat, with the army boat. With the and they make me do work because I didn't pay for the fare, for the voyage. I came in the army, and they make me to paint the-- the-- the ship every morning. To paint, to paint-- sometimes, I got sick and tired. I took Saba on my hand. And I say, the baby's crying. I got to get down from work a little bit. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so do you remember where you arrived in New York? Where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived, I mean, I don't-- I don't know where. I came right from-- in New York. I know-- I know where this was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Atlanta? Did you drive-- or train?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who picked you up at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Federation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that-- when you-- how did you-- did they find a job for you here in Atlanta? How did they-- how did you get-- start making a living here in Atlanta?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I get-- make a living in Atlanta? You see, I came here with my-- my-- my brother was immigration from before. And here we got a free loan association. You know what is-- you know a free loan, what common refugees like me when they needed help, or you're going to go in a little business or something like this, they make you a loan with no interest, you know. The Federation will give you a loan, too. I-- I remember they gave me $1,500 loan from the free loan, and the Federation give me $500 or $800, I don't remember. And we bought a little grocery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, but why it was grocery store? Because they know that kind of business. A grocery store is everybody going in and they're taking what's it-- looking what they wanted. And-- and that's easy. You don't have to talk, nothing in the world only-- but you've got to know somebody else. In Europe, you never see-- I never see in my life in Germany cans, canned goods. Food in cans-- in Europe, you never-- never see that much.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I got to learn. What I did, I took a piece of paper, and I write in Yiddish. I write in Yiddish, like, say, pork and beans, [SPEAKING YIDDISH] you know, chicken noodle soup, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], you know, in Yiddish. And I make-- I make a long list, you know, canned goods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if somebody came for pork and beans, I forgot where I put it, so in the middle or in the front. And It took me a half hour till I found out where this is. He's-- my customer, he says, I come back. Don't you worry, I come back, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I care the first business, in grocery store, and I stayed 33 years. And this is the way I raised my children. We-- we-- we trying to make a living. And I paid off the free loan. I pay off the-- the Federation. And they never believe I can be able to pay them back sometimes. But I did as the first thing not to have-- And things got-- I got good children. I got good grandchildren. I got good [INAUDIBLE]. And this is my life. And I had a good wife, pleasure to maintain.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you think that your experience in the Holocaust-- how did that affect your children? Did you ever talk about it with them when they were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I talk and my wife-- my wife got mad on me, but I telling the children. She say, if you went through certain things, the children don't have to know about them. The children do not-- and this is wrong. But the children later find out more how I told them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sure do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not religious extremely, but I sure do. I remember from [INAUDIBLE] synagogue. And I-- I-- I-- I doing everything, all the-- all the-- the-- the-- the seders with the-- with the holidays, with everything, a religious thing. I make it by myself the [INAUDIBLE]. The children are making [INAUDIBLE] you know? And even my grandchildren are doing the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that you survived the Holocaust? Why do you think you're living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this is-- do you believe in miracles? And I believe now in a miracle. I talked to Rabbi Epstein, if you know him, from our synagogue, the first rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say, Rabbi, where are the miracles what God make before, thousands of years ago, how they-- they-- came out-- all the Jewish people came out from Egypt. And miracles like, maybe, Jonah. Jonah, when he-- when the-- a big fish swallow him and-- and something else, all kind of miracles. Why he didn't make a miracle when he see how people killing millions of children, children what they never see the world yet? He say to me, a miracle? But you became alive. That's a miracle, he told me. He said, this is a miracle. If not, there wouldn't be a miracle what you didn't be alive. This is what he told me. And I believe. I-- I-- I don't care if somebody don't believe in nothing. I don't care for certain people. People got to believe in something, not only Jewish people, but he got to believe in something. People, what they don't believe, they are not-- And for me, I haven't any-- any-- anything for them, you know? What do you have to say to your grandchildren now about the Holocaust? What sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter and my son-in-law, took the children-- they came yesterday-- from the Holocaust Museum in Washington. And we went-- the children they been with me on the opening. I've been on the opening for the Holocaust Museum in Washington. But the children didn't see this. And then Marcia, with my daughter, want these children, to bring them in Washington to see the-- the Holocaust Museum, how came-- how I became alive and how I got liberated and everything-- by the Dachau, by the camps, with the ghettos, with everything in there. You've never been there in the Holocaust Museum. That's wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you have to say-- what does Mr. Sam Wise have to say about what's important to him now to tell your-- I'm sure you've told your children, but to your grandchildren? And how would you like to be remembered?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- how--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to tell them? What would you like them to remember about the Holocaust? What kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell them-- I brought in all the children [INAUDIBLE]. I brought them in Israel. We've been at Yad Vashem. This is a Holocaust, you know, this is the main Holocaust from everything. When the children see this, the grandson, on when we came out from the Yad Vashem-- and the children hugged me so tight for the hand. When we walked out back from there, and they looked me over, I see every minute. Papa, in the eyes. Papa-- with tears in the eyes-- Papa, Papa. They never believed this until they see this-- everything. Papa, Papa, [INAUDIBLE] the little bitty one. They hold me so tight. This-- this-- this is unbelievable. And to see how I came out with tears. And they're crying, and Papa, and Papa, and Papa. And that's what they're doing now. And they understand, and they know. At least mine-- grandson, the little one, he's going to be now bar mitzvah in next year. He write in the high school my life from-- from the concentration camp. But he doesn't know too much about the concentration camp. But 'Papa's Life,' he write it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything else to say about anything-- in concerning the Holocaust, or anything at all about your life that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my life is that I want to see the world got to know and got to go to see the Holocaust Museum. And not to forget, to never happens this again. And this is my-- my-- the main thing what I want to tell. And I got to tell the world because the world got to know what happens-- what-- what the Germans' culture did. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now we'll stop tape, I guess. [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling. You know much time they want on this?

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time do we have left on this?

speaker: interviewer
text: About 20 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good, OK. [PAUSES FOR 38 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mr. Wise, if you could just look in there, and you can tell us who everybody is that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I've been in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but tell us where is this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Kovno, my-- my-- my sister's wedding. She's the bride here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you can go ahead and point it. And tell us who everybody is.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I've got-- I've got to get closer a little bit, all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Ruven. And these are my aunts and uncles. On the-- on the-- this is my sister, what she got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is my mother, an this is my father. And what's their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham. This is-- this is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Batya. Very easy. And this is my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- her name is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lea, I think. And this is my cousins. And this is my uncles-- Uncle Yoshe. And this is my uncle, too, my-- my father's brother, Uncle Judel. And this is my brother-in-law, Sholem. And this is my sister-- the other sister, Asna. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this is my sister-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my brother-- my sister-in-law, Rajzel. And this is my brother, Issak. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the others I couldn't remember already, this is so long. This is my cousins-- or my cousins or my aunts--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I couldn't remember. My cousin. We're cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a cousin, too. And this is my sister-in-law's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how-- And did you say who this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin, too, but don't remember names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that you have this photograph? How did you get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister from South Africa had it. So she sent this to-- to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so she left to go to South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: She left right before the war. She married in Kovno. And she left right after the wedding. They left in South Africa. That's what she survived. If not, she would be killed, too, like everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're moving on to the next photograph here. Oops, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us where this was taken and who they-- the people are, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken here in-- in-- in Atlanta. And this is my sister, and this is Saba. And this is Eli, and this is Helen. This is my brother's two children, twins. This is Saba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Atlanta, Georgia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Atlanta, when she came in from Africa to visit me the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the year-- '40-- about-- about '58, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Wise, who is the-- who are these people?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my brother in Munich. This is in 1948, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Issak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you on the right or the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I am from the left. This is me, And this is my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mr. Wise, tell us about that photograph that we're looking at now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is where the American Army came in Munich in the concentration-- in-- in Munich. And they make pictures piled from town to town, dead bodies on the street, some of them moving. And they would say all the time, maybe we can bring somebody alive. And they took out three from the whole group dead people-- me and two more. Matter of fact, when I came in Atlanta, I found the third man, what the-- the third man what he-- what he became alive. He had an apartment here in Atlanta. And he recognized me. I recognized him. And we fall both together crying and laughing. And this is a picture from this what-- the major, an American major, gave me the whole album.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph, do you know what year this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was the year-- when they came in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the major thinks that you were part of the bodies underneath them?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why these gentlemen-- that's why they give me the album. But that's what about. As a matter of fact, what I got from-- from Milwaukee, from one man what he call me, and he say, I am your liberator. And he came here on the gathering with his wife. And he brought all the pictures, the same what-- what is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Mr. Wise, what are we looking at now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my driver's license for the guard in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We calling this [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is June 30, 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wise, what is this picture, please? And tell us who the people are.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people-- this is my sister in the middle. And this is my sister-in-law. And this is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '48, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this Munich or Atlanta, Georgia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, in Atlanta, I mean. I am sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is in Atlanta, Georgia, after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Right Thank you. Tell me when.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us who everybody is, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. First of all, this is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida. And this is me, Sam Wise. And this is my grandson, Donald. And this is my granddaughter, Stacy. And this is my grandson, Zach-- Jeremy. And this is my grandson, Zachary. And this is my grandson, Todd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph taken about-- I think about a-- about a year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. We are now. We could do it. You want me to?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody look at the camera. Mr. Wise, tell us who everybody is and what you'd like to say to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I've got to say to them? I got to say this-- I want to be alive till all my grandchildren get married. Is that a nice wish? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is everybody in the-- in the picture here?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my daughters and my grandchildren, except my son-in-laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us who everybody is?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. From the right side is Saba. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From the left hand is Marcia. My first grandson is Donald. The second one is Stacy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The third one is Jeremy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The fourth one is Zachary. And the last one, Todd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now somebody--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all I got. But this is my person. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say that, I hope that after reviewing this tape and that all the children will see it, it will be something that will be carried down from generation to generation and from our children to our grandchildren. And the only one that's visibly missing is my mother, but I know that she's here with us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, everybody.
